text: She's a 69-year-old woman who presented with a hilar left upper lobe lesion that was incidentally discovered.
She has a history of a heart attack before, has indwelling coronary stents, is a former smoker, and quit 10 years ago.
She has some other minor comorbidities, and we've cleared her for surgery by doing pulmonary function testing.
We've also had her evaluated by the pulmonary team, where she underwent an endobronchial ultrasound-guided needle aspiration of stations 4R and 4L lymph nodes that were both negative for malignancy.
The patient has now been cleared for surgery, including COVID-19 PCR and antibody testing.
Today we'll be performing a video-assisted thoracoscopic left upper lobectomy.
We'll start with three small ports that are 10 millimeters in size.
Once we provide that there is no evidence of metastatic disease, we'll enlarge to a utility port and then we'll put a protector on the port and proceed with taking the left upper lobe of the lung.
Because the lesion is in the hilum, we always talk about the possibility of conversion if we cannot do the best cancer surgery with a minimally invasive approach.
